does: This paper reframes clinician overrides of AI recommendations as preference signals for training clinical decision support systems. The authors develop a formal framework that categorizes overrides into five types, each targeting specific model improvements. They propose a preference learning formulation that conditions on patient state, organizational context, and clinician capability (decomposed into execution and alignment dimensions). The core technical contribution is a dual learning architecture that jointly trains a reward model and capability model through alternating optimization, designed to prevent suppression bias—a failure mode where the system systematically suppresses correct-but-difficult recommendations when clinician execution capability is insufficient. The framework is motivated by value-based care settings where observable outcomes make this signal structure particularly rich and actionable.
connects: This work builds directly on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and preference learning literature, but specializes these methods for clinical settings where expert annotators make decisions with real consequences and long-term outcomes are measurable. It extends prior work on learning from human feedback by modeling the annotator's capabilities as part of the learning problem rather than treating all overrides as uniform signals. The paper connects to literature on distributional shift and covariate shift in ML, addressing how clinician capability variations create implicit distribution mismatch. It also engages with research on human-AI collaboration, building on prior work that studies when and why humans override automated recommendations, but formalizes this through a structured preference learning lens.
enables: This framework enables more robust clinical AI systems that improve through deployment rather than degrading when deployed to clinicians with varying capabilities. By explicitly modeling suppression bias, it opens pathways to systems that can identify which recommendations require additional decision support or human expertise, rather than abandoning sound guidance when facing execution challenges. The formalization of override signals could extend to other high-stakes domains with expert users and observable outcomes—medical imaging, drug discovery, and scientific research. The capability-conditioned preference learning approach provides a foundation for personalized AI training where systems adapt to individual expert profiles. It also suggests new evaluation metrics for clinical AI systems beyond standard accuracy, grounding assessment in organizational and clinician heterogeneity typical of real healthcare deployment.
